letters_page: Gary R. Spiewak writes in to note that the powers of the Squadron Sinister from AVEN #70 seem to be "taken directly from your chief competitor's best known mags. Not only are their power almost exactly the same as your competitor's heroes' powers, but the origins of these characters are also very similar to those of your competitor." He's not complaining, he says, only making an observation. As part of Marvel's response, they claim they took the matter to Roy Thomas, whose response was "I refuse to answer on the grounds..." Then he took off and we haven't seen him since, so it looks like we'll never know...! John Schuch looked at the Whizzer from AVEN #70 and figured that Marvel was just reusing their old character from the 40s. And he suggests that Nighthawk at least would perhaps work well as an antagonist in the pages of CAP or DD. It just so happens that this month's DD features that very thing! Harold Colwest mentions a continuity flaw that I myself mentioned after reading the recent IRON #19 and AVEN #69 books, which was related to the use of Captain America, and specifically how he was still present in 1969 after Tony Stark's operation. Marvel's response indicated that the Captain America we see in the Iron Man issue was from after all the business with Kang and the Grandmaster was cleared up, suggesting that this may not be a particularly satisfactory explanation - but at least we tried. I have to agree that this is not at all a satisfactory explanation, especially since Iron Man himself took part in the Kang/Grandmaster affair, appearing in AVEN #70. Are you telling me now that Cap took the time to travel into the past to announce the success of Tony's operation when he returned to 1969 - with Iron Man by his side? Ugh!
